data.title: UK 10-Year Gilt Yield Rises to 5-Week High
data.description:
The UK 10-year gilt yield climbed to 4.6%, the highest in five weeks, as budget uncertainty overshadowed rising expectations of a December rate cut.

With the government entering a period of intense speculation over its upcoming budget, investors face a murkier backdrop until clearer fiscal plans emerge.

PM Keir Starmer vowed there will be no austerity and that the budget will reflect Labour values, while Chancellor Rachel Reeves aims to ease living costs and further cool inflation in her 26 November statement.

October data showed headline inflation easing, though stubborn food prices remain a concern.

Even so, traders boosted bets on monetary easing, with markets pricing an 84% chance of a quarter-point cut in December.

data.title: UK to ban the resale of tickets for profit to protect fans
data.description:
Britain said on Wednesday it would ban the resale of tickets to concerts, sport and other live events for profit, disrupting ticket touts and the platforms that benefit from their activities.

Culture Secretary Lisa Nandy said touts were ripping off fans by using bots to snap up batches of tickets for coveted shows and reselling them at sky-high prices.

"Our new proposals will shut down the touts' racket and make world-class music, comedy, theatre and sport affordable for everyone," she said, after the government had promised action.

Tickets for tours by Harry Styles, Taylor Swift, Oasis and others have been offered on resale sites for hugely inflated prices minutes after they sell out.

Touts use sophisticated automated "bots" to beat online queues, the government said.

Shares in U.S. company StubHub, the owner of resale site Viagogo, fell 14% on Monday after the plan was reported.

The government had consulted on setting a cap of up to 30% above face value for the resale of tickets.

But it said on Wednesday that resales above face value - defined as original ticket price plus unavoidable fees - would be illegal.

Service fees charged by platforms will also be capped to prevent the ban being undermined, it said.

The new rules will apply to any platform reselling tickets to UK fans, including secondary ticketing platforms and social media websites, it said. Businesses who break the regulations could face financial penalties of up to 10% of global turnover.

Viagogo said on Tuesday that processes to verify tickets would be a more effective way to stop illegal activity.

"Evidence shows price caps have repeatedly failed fans. In countries like Ireland and Australia, fraud rates are nearly four times higher than in the UK as price caps push consumers towards unregulated sites," a Viagogo spokesperson said.

"Opening the market to greater competition also helps drive prices down, benefiting fans."

data.title: The Private Wellness Clubs That Charge Members Up to Thousands a Month — WSJ
data.description:
By Sara Ashley O'Brien

Tomas El Rayess wasn't looking to join another private club.

"I've been a member of a country club and I've been a member of Soho House, and I was never fully satisfied," says El Rayess, 49, a culinary medicine chef in Los Angeles. "It was great socially, but it involved only socializing." Everything revolved around alcohol, he says; he wanted a place where he could meet new people and improve his overall well-being. Less drinking, more cold-plunging.

When Hume opened in his Venice neighborhood in 2024, he found what he had been missing. Part gym, part spa and part social club, the space is designed for the wellness-minded and aesthetically conscious. "A luxurious sanctuary with equipment that looks like art" is how a local recommendations site describes it.

At $450 a month with a $500 initiation fee, Hume is pricier than Equinox, but it's also something new: a private wellness club built for the era of self-optimization and sober curiosity. Similar businesses are popping up all over the country, pitching themselves as one-stop shops for workouts, recovery and healthy fun. Membership runs from hundreds to thousands of dollars a month — and that's assuming you make it through the application process.

At New York's Continuum, where membership starts at $40,000 a year, founder and CEO Jeff Halevy says a committee vets applicants to "make sure that they're aligned with the club, with the health philosophy and in the cultural ethos." New members go through an onboarding process that includes "performance-based bloodwork," a DEXA scan to measure body composition and bone density and metabolic panels. They also receive an Oura ring; Continuum uses the information it gathers to help members optimize their workouts and routines.

Most of these clubs do not request blood samples. They typically ask applicants for links to social-media accounts, short bios and referrals from current members. El Rayess says when he applied to Hume in fall 2024, the process involved a simple questionnaire and a tour of the space, after which he joined for $395 a month with a $500 initiation fee. Now, in addition to hot-yoga classes, core and mobility exercises in the gym, and cycling between the sauna, steam room and cold plunge, he gets writing done at the rooftop cafe and attends Saturday socials, where members are allowed to bring guests.

Business owners are betting that the wealthy and wellness-obsessed will pay more for an elevated, social experience.

"Instead of going into a gym, where you can smell rubber and hear people grunting, I wanted to have it feel like a mix between a home and an art gallery," says Sandy Bole, the co-founder of Hume, over a foam-top pistachio-milk matcha. "The home is really welcoming and cozy, and the art gallery is inspiring and a little bit more edgy." The name stems from the French word humer, which means to sense or smell.

"It's gotta be warm and almost womblike in a way that you feel very appeased as soon as you walk in, and then you bring your guard down," says Bole, a native of France. He says that the club recently capped its membership to make sure the space doesn't feel crowded.

When Krista Berlincourt, 39, first heard about Hume, she was skeptical. "I was wary this would feel like an Instagrammy cesspool," she says. "Genuinely, I was worried it would be full of people on their phones." (There are no phones allowed in Hume's classes, she's since learned.)

But Hume won her over when several of her favorite instructors from other studios joined to teach. Being part of a club has given her back some of the perks of a full-time job, like watercooler conversation.

"That combined with the vibe of a spa," she says.

Los Angeles has become a playground of private wellness clubs: Three miles north of Hume is the Proper Hotel in Santa Monica, which launched its own club in March. Members pay $6,000 annually (and a $2,500 initiation fee) for access to its newly revamped gym, recovery suite, lounge and fitness classes.

Brad Korzen and Brian De Lowe, who co-founded the Proper with Alex Samek, decided to open the hotel's wellness offerings to nonguests after the Palisades fire. People who had evacuated their homes and temporarily moved into the hotel wanted continued access to its top-of-the-line facilities after they left. As wellness enthusiasts themselves, Korzen and De Lowe got it — and the idea of another revenue stream certainly didn't hurt.

Santa Monica's Proper Club has just under 100 members to date. A second location at the Proper Hotel in Austin, Texas, has about 75 members. Both clubs intend to cap membership at around 200. There is a waitlist, the founders say; prospective members must meet with the club team in person before they're accepted.

Before the Proper's club opened, content creator Julia Corot says she visited the San Vicente Bungalows, a private club with locations in Los Angeles and New York. "It's more of a restaurant," she says, with lounges where people can work or chat. What she wanted was a space where she could socialize and "play sport."

Heimat, the West Hollywood application-only wellness club, appealed to her. In addition to group classes, a Himalayan salt dry sauna, and a recovery and beauty spa, there's a rooftop pool and a restaurant that's open to the public. Membership runs $350 monthly (with a $350 initiation fee), but Corot says "it's too far away for me to cross the 405 [freeway] every day." ("Many of our members that live across town tell us they find the drive worthwhile because it becomes their one destination for everything they need in a day," a Heimat spokesperson says.)

Corot, 40, instead joined the Proper's club as a founding member, paying $5,000 for the first year, plus the $2,500 initiation fee and taxes. "It's a social club, but more for singles," she says.

In New York, the lifestyle brand Kith is opening a members-only padel and wellness club in Greenwich Village called Kith Ivy. It will feature a fitness center and a Giorgio Armani luxury spa with high-end treatments, as well as a Jacuzzi, sauna, steam rooms and cold plunges. Members can nosh on Moroccan Mediterranean fare from Cafe Mogador in the "living room" of the space. There will also be a "tonic bar" from Erewhon, featuring the gourmet L.A. grocer's famously expensive and elaborate smoothies.

Also in the neighborhood is Continuum, which opened in September 2024.

"I mean this in the most humble way possible, but what we're doing is one of one," says Continuum's Halevy. The brand made a splash when it launched thanks to its eye-popping $100,000 annual membership — a price that makes the current starting rate of $40,000 sound reasonable. "I didn't wake up one morning and say to myself, My next business is going to be yet another place where wealthy people can hang out," Halevy says.

Continuum sees itself as a tech company first. "We've got a proprietary technology that takes in an unparalleled stack of biometric data and is able to use that data to drive the member journey," Halevy says.

That data allows the club to make precise recommendations. "Your poor night's sleep can literally change the amount of weight on a single set that you might be doing in your exercise program the very next day," Halevy says.

The facility offers premium gym equipment, full-body red-light therapy, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, a Finnish sauna, cold plunges, a float therapy pool, and a team of coaches and clinicians to assist members at no extra cost.

"Our model is all-inclusive," he says. Entrées, light bites and smoothies are all available on-site for members at no extra cost. Halevy says the club's capacity is 250 members, which it expects to hit in 2026. He's already eyeing a second location.

Write to Sara Ashley O'Brien at sara.obrien@wsj.com

data.title: Silver IRA Rollover Guide Released (2025 Update)
data.description:
IRAEmpire has released a new and updated guide on Silver IRA rollover to explain how the process works and what to look out for.

TALLAHASSEE, FLORIDA / ACCESS Newswire / November 19, 2025 / Diversifying your retirement savings has never been more important. With rising market volatility, inflation pressure, and global financial uncertainty, many investors are turning to alternative assets-especially precious metals. Among these options, a Silver IRA rollover has become an increasingly popular strategy for individuals who want to protect and strengthen their long-term financial future.

Matthew Hunt, Senior Write at IRAEmpire says, "This comprehensive guide explores everything you need to know about rolling over an existing retirement account into a Silver IRA, including benefits, risks, IRS rules, and a ranked list of the best providers to help you get started."

Overview of Best Silver IRA Rollover Companies:

  • Augusta Precious Metals (Best for Large Investments)

  • Lear Capital (Best Customer Support)

  • Goldencrest Metals (Best Prices)

What Is a Silver IRA?

A Silver IRA is a type of Self-Directed Individual Retirement Account (IRA) that allows investors to hold physical silver-such as bullion bars, rounds, and certain government-minted coins-inside a tax-advantaged retirement account. Unlike traditional IRAs, which primarily include stocks, bonds, and mutual funds, a Silver IRA gives you the ability to diversify into tangible precious metals that have historically acted as long-term stores of value. These accounts operate under the same IRS rules as any other IRA, but they provide access to assets that are typically unavailable in standard brokerage accounts.

To qualify for inclusion in an IRA, silver must meet strict IRS purity requirements. Specifically, silver bullion must be at least .999 fine, and coins must be produced by an approved government mint or accredited refiner. Popular IRA-eligible options include American Silver Eagles, Canadian Silver Maple Leafs, and .999+ silver bars from LBMA- or COMEX-approved refiners. Collectibles, numismatic coins, and jewelry do not qualify because they carry variable premiums and do not meet federal purity and authenticity standards.

A Silver IRA is held through a Self-Directed IRA custodian-usually a trust company or financial institution that specializes in alternative assets. This custodian is responsible for handling paperwork, maintaining compliance, and ensuring your physical silver is stored in an IRS-approved depository. These depositories provide secure, insured vault storage that preserves the tax-advantaged status of your account.

By allowing direct ownership of physical silver, a Silver IRA gives investors a hedge against inflation, economic uncertainty, and currency weakening. For individuals seeking an asset that combines intrinsic value with long-term diversification benefits, a Silver IRA can be a strategic addition to a broader retirement plan.

Find the Best Silver IRA Rollover Company of Your State

How a Silver IRA Rollover Works

A Silver IRA rollover is the process of transferring funds from an existing retirement account-such as a 401(k), 403(b), TSP, SEP IRA, SIMPLE IRA, or Traditional IRA-into a new Self-Directed IRA that can legally hold physical silver. While the concept is simple, the rollover must be executed correctly to avoid taxes, penalties, or delays. Most reputable Silver IRA companies coordinate the entire procedure for you, but it's important to understand how it works.

The rollover begins by selecting a Self-Directed IRA custodian and opening your new account. Once the account is active, your new custodian will request a transfer of funds from your current retirement plan. Depending on the type of plan you have, this may involve completing a transfer request form, contacting your former plan administrator, or verifying eligibility requirements-especially for employer-sponsored plans like 401(k)s, which sometimes require separation from the employer before rollovers are allowed.

Types of Rollovers

1. Direct Rollover (Preferred Method)

In a direct rollover, funds move electronically from your existing retirement account straight to your new Silver IRA custodian.

Advantages include:

  • No tax withholding

  • No early withdrawal penalties

  • Simplified, IRS-compliant process

    Because the money never touches your hands, this method is the safest and most common.

2. Indirect Rollover

With an indirect rollover, funds are paid out to you first, and you have 60 days to deposit them into your new IRA.

Risks include:

  • Mandatory 20% tax withholding from employer plans

  • Immediate taxation if you miss the 60-day deadline

  • Possible 10% early withdrawal penalty

    Because of these risks, most custodians strongly advise choosing a direct rollover unless absolutely necessary.

Benefits of a Silver IRA Rollover

Rolling your retirement assets into a Silver IRA comes with several unique benefits that can strengthen your long-term financial strategy.

1. Hedge Against Inflation

Silver has historically maintained its purchasing power, even during periods of high inflation. When fiat currency declines in value, precious metals often rise.

2. Portfolio Diversification

Most retirement accounts are heavily weighted toward stocks and bonds. Adding silver provides a non-correlated asset, which means it behaves differently from traditional markets.

3. Economic Downturn Protection

During recessions or market corrections, physical silver can act as a defensive asset. It is frequently viewed as a safe haven when equities struggle.

4. Strong Industrial Demand

Silver plays an essential role in solar energy, electronics, and medical technology industries. Rising industrial demand can support long-term value.

5. Tangible Asset Ownership

Unlike digital or paper-based investments, silver offers physical, intrinsic value. That level of security appeals to investors seeking stability.

Potential Risks and Considerations

No investment is without risk. Before committing to a Silver IRA rollover, it's important to understand potential downsides:

1. Silver Price Volatility

Silver prices can fluctuate significantly over short periods, often more sharply than gold.

2. Storage and Custodial Fees

Approved silver must be stored in an IRS-approved depository, which involves annual fees for storage and insurance.

3. Liquidity Considerations

Although silver is generally easy to buy and sell, liquidating large amounts may require working through your custodian.

4. Choosing a Reputable Provider

The precious metals industry varies widely in quality. Working with a trustworthy, transparent provider is essential to protecting your investment.

Step-by-Step Guide to Completing a Silver IRA Rollover

If you're ready to move forward, here's how the process typically works:

1. Select an IRS-Approved Custodian

Self-directed IRAs must be managed by a qualified custodian, such as a trust company or financial institution.

2. Choose a Silver IRA Company

Your chosen provider will coordinate the purchase, paperwork, storage setup, and rollover procedure.

3. Open the New Self-Directed IRA

Your provider will guide you through the account setup.

4. Initiate the Rollover

Your new custodian will request the transfer of funds from your existing retirement provider.

5. Fund the IRA

Once the transfer is complete, you'll have funds available to purchase silver.

6. Select Approved Silver Products

Choose from IRS-approved coins and bullion. Your provider will help ensure compliance.

7. Secure Depository Storage

Your metals will be shipped to an IRS-approved depository for secure storage.

8. Monitor Your Investment

Like any retirement asset, silver should be periodically reviewed as part of your broader financial plan.

Learn More on How to Open a Silver IRA with Experts

IRS Rules for Silver IRA Rollovers

Because Silver IRAs involve physical assets, the IRS has specific rules to maintain tax-advantaged status:

Approved Silver Types

  • .999+ silver bars

  • IRA-approved rounds

  • Specific government-minted coins

Non-Approved Items

  • Collectible or numismatic coins

  • Jewelry

  • Non-pure or uncertified silver

Other Key Rules

  • Rollovers must comply with the 60-day rule (for indirect rollovers)

  • Only one rollover per 12-month period per IRA

  • Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) apply to Silver IRAs beginning at age 73

  • Home storage is not permitted-metals must be stored in an approved depository

Failure to comply with IRS regulations can lead to taxes, penalties, or disqualification of the IRA.

Best Providers for Silver IRA Rollover (Ranked)

Choosing the right provider can greatly influence your experience and overall satisfaction with a Silver IRA. Below are the top providers ranked in the order you requested.

1. Augusta Precious Metals - Best Overall

Augusta Precious Metals consistently ranks as one of the most reputable and transparent precious metals IRA companies in the United States. The company is known for its client-first philosophy, exceptional education resources, and clear fee structure.

Key Highlights

  • Lifetime customer support

  • Transparent, no-pressure sales approach

  • Comprehensive educational materials & one-on-one web conferences

  • High customer ratings across independent review platforms

Augusta also maintains strong relationships with IRS-approved custodians and depositories, making the rollover process smooth and efficient. Their commitment to transparency and client protection earns them the top spot.

Read Augusta Precious Metals Reviews, Fees and More

2. Lear Capital - Strong Industry Track Record

Lear Capital is a well-known name in the precious metals investment space, offering a wide range of IRA-approved silver products. The company provides detailed market insights and real-time pricing information, which appeals to investors who want to stay actively informed.

Key Highlights

  • Longstanding presence in the industry

  • Wide selection of silver coins and bars

  • Dedicated IRA rollover specialists

  • Online account tools for tracking performance

Lear Capital's streamlined onboarding process and industry experience make it a solid option for investors seeking variety and educational resources.

Read Lear Capital Reviews, Fees and More

3. Goldencrest Metals - Emerging but Promising

Goldencrest Metals is a newer but rapidly growing precious metals firm that has gained attention for its competitive pricing and customer-friendly support. The company focuses on simplifying the process for first-time precious metals investors.

Key Highlights

  • Competitive dealer pricing

  • Transparent, user-friendly process

  • Strong customer service

  • Offers a range of IRA-approved silver products

While Goldencrest is newer than some of its competitors, its growing reputation and investor-friendly approach make it a strong contender for those seeking personalized guidance.

Read Goldencrest Metals Reviews, Fees and More

Choosing the Right Custodian and Depository

Once you select a provider, the next important decision is choosing a custodian and storage solution.

Custodian Considerations

Look for custodians that offer:

  • Experience in self-directed IRAs

  • Transparent fee structures

  • Strong compliance record

  • Helpful customer support

Depository Options

Precious metals must be stored in an IRS-approved facility. Two main types of storage exist:

1. Segregated Storage

Your metals are kept separately from others' holdings.

2. Non-Segregated (Commingled) Storage

Your metals are stored with other clients' metals but still accounted for.

Both options are insured and secure, but segregated storage typically costs more.

Types of Silver Eligible for IRA Rollovers

The IRS restricts Silver IRA holdings to specific forms and purities. Some common eligible products include:

  • American Silver Eagle coins

  • Canadian Silver Maple Leaf coins

  • .999+ silver bullion bars from accredited refiners

  • IRA-approved silver rounds

Items such as collectible coins, commemorative issues, or rare numismatics are not eligible because the IRS views them as collectibles rather than investment bullion.

Fees Involved in a Silver IRA Rollover

Costs vary by provider, but you can generally expect the following:

1. Account Setup Fees

A one-time fee to open the self-directed IRA.

2. Annual Custodian Fees

Covers account maintenance and regulatory compliance.

3. Storage Fees

Charged by the depository for holding and insuring your silver.

4. Dealer Premiums

The markup over spot price for coins and bars.

5. Miscellaneous Fees

Such as wire fees, processing fees, or early closure fees.

Transparent providers will clearly outline these costs before you commit.

Silver IRA Rollover vs. Gold IRA Rollover

Both Gold and Silver IRAs provide long-term portfolio diversification, inflation protection, and the security of holding tangible assets, but each metal behaves differently and serves a unique role within a retirement strategy. Understanding the distinctions can help investors choose the right mix.

Silver Advantages

Silver is often considered the "affordable powerhouse" of precious metals. Its lower price point allows investors to accumulate more ounces for the same dollar amount, which can be appealing for those starting with smaller rollover balances. Silver also has significant industrial utility-particularly in solar panels, electronics, automotive components, and medical technology. This industrial demand can boost silver's performance during periods of technological expansion or green-energy investment. Historically, silver has also shown stronger percentage gains during precious metal bull markets, giving it greater upside potential, albeit with more volatility.

Gold Advantages

Gold is typically viewed as the foundation of a precious metals IRA because of its long-standing reputation as a safe haven. It tends to remain more stable during market turbulence and is less influenced by industrial trends. Gold's global recognition and higher liquidity make it easier to sell quickly, which is important for retirees planning future distributions. While gold does not usually experience the same rapid surges as silver, its lower volatility and consistent demand offer reliable long-term preservation of wealth.

Because silver and gold excel in different economic environments, many retirement investors choose to hold both metals, creating a balanced blend of stability (gold) and growth potential (silver) within their IRA.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. Missing the 60-Day Deadline

For indirect rollovers, failing to deposit funds in time can result in taxes and penalties.

2. Purchasing Non-Eligible Products

Collectible or non-pure items can disqualify your IRA.

3. Failing to Compare Fees

High or hidden fees can erode your returns over time.

4. Choosing an Unreliable Provider

Work only with firms that are transparent, well-reviewed, and compliant.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I roll over a 401(k) into a Silver IRA?

Yes. Most employer-sponsored plans allow rollovers into self-directed IRAs once you meet eligibility requirements.

Are Silver IRA profits taxed?

Earnings grow tax-deferred (Traditional IRA) or tax-free (Roth IRA), depending on your account type.

Can I take physical possession of my silver?

Yes, but only after you reach retirement age and take a distribution.

How much silver can I hold in an IRA?

There is no specific silver limit-only standard IRS contribution and rollover limits apply.

Find the Best Gold and Silver IRA Company of Your State

Conclusion

A Silver IRA rollover allows investors to transfer funds from an existing retirement account-such as a 401(k), 403(b), TSP, or traditional IRA-into a Self-Directed IRA that holds physical silver. This strategy has grown in popularity as inflation, market volatility, and global uncertainty push many people to seek more stable, tangible assets.

A Silver IRA offers the security of physical silver combined with the tax advantages of a traditional retirement account. In a Silver IRA, investors can own IRS-approved silver bullion or coins that meet .999 purity standards and are produced by accredited mints. Unlike conventional IRAs that focus on stocks and bonds, this structure provides direct exposure to physical silver.

Rolling over funds into a Silver IRA can be done through either a direct or indirect rollover, though the direct method is preferred because it allows custodians to transfer funds between accounts without taxes or penalties. Indirect rollovers require the investor to redeposit funds within 60 days, which can be risky if the deadline is missed.

Silver IRAs offer strong diversification benefits, protecting savings from inflation and economic downturns while benefiting from rising industrial demand for silver in technology, medical equipment, and renewable energy. However, investors should be aware of silver's price volatility and the custodial and storage fees required by the IRS, since all metals must be stored in an approved depository.

Choosing a reliable provider is essential for a smooth rollover, and top companies include Augusta Precious Metals, known for transparent pricing, lifetime support, and exceptional customer education; Lear Capital, a long-established provider offering robust product choices and experienced IRA specialists; and Goldencrest Metals, a newer but fast-growing firm with competitive pricing and supportive guidance for first-time investors.

IRS rules also require that only approved silver products be purchased for an IRA, and metals cannot be stored at home. Standard IRA regulations still apply, including contribution limits and Required Minimum Distributions at retirement age. Overall, a Silver IRA rollover can strengthen a retirement portfolio by adding a tangible, inflation-resistant asset, and with the right provider and proper compliance, it offers a secure and effective way to diversify long-term savings.

About IRAEmpire

IRAEmpire is a leading online resource dedicated to helping investors make smarter decisions about retirement planning, precious metals, and alternative investments. Built with a mission to simplify complex financial topics, IRAEmpire provides in-depth reviews, comparison guides, and educational content focused on Gold IRAs, Silver IRAs, and other self-directed retirement accounts.

Key features include detailed breakdowns of top Gold IRA companies, step-by-step investment guides, and market trend analysis. By combining data-driven research with easy-to-understand explanations, IRAEmpire empowers readers to compare providers, evaluate fees, and choose the right partner for their long-term financial security.

Whether you're a first-time investor exploring precious metals or an experienced retiree looking to diversify, IRAEmpire serves as a trusted guide. Its goal is simple: to help you protect and grow your wealth through smart, informed retirement planning.

Aura Renewable Acquisitions plc

19 November 2025 - The Chairman of Aura Renewable Acquisitions Plc (the 'Company') announces the appointments of Suresh Withana and Philip Pooley as Directors of the Company with effect from 19 November 2025.

With over 25 years of investing and investment banking experience, Suresh Withana was formerly the Global Head of Special Situations at Tikehau Capital, a founding member of Mizuho International Plc's Global Special Situations Group and a senior professional in the Investment Banking divisions of Merrill Lynch International and Bank of America.

Phil Pooley has advised on over 50 IPOs on the London Stock Exchange, including both AIM and the Main Market. He has also been involved in listing companies on NASDAQ and in Hong Kong. He was also a Partner at one of London's larger Corporate Brokers, Shard Capital, raising capital for international companies listed on the London Stock Exchange.

I look forward to working with the new Directors on bringing a suitable transaction to the Company and thereby maximising value for our shareholders.

data.title: In a U.S. First, New Mexico Opens Doors to Free Child Care for All — WSJ
data.description:
By Harriet Torry and Dan Frosch | Photography by Ramsay de Give for WSJ

One of the nation's poorest states has launched one of its most ambitious social policies: universal child care.

New Mexico this month became the first state to guarantee free care, regardless of income, starting at six weeks of age. The state says the plan — covered largely by a fund set up with oil-and-gas revenues — should save families roughly $16,000 a year on average on full-time daycare bills.

The initiative expands on free child care New Mexico has provided for people under an income cap since 2022, a program that cost the state $345 million in the last fiscal year. The state estimates the expanding program will cost $445 million in the current fiscal year, then $600 million in the next year.

The program aims to get more people into the workforce and improve child development. The state, which has long struggled with high levels of poverty and poor educational rankings, also wants to attract and keep families with young children.

"We're top of the bad list, whether it's crime or whether it's high school education outcomes, whether it's reading proficiency, whether it's college graduation," said New Mexico House speaker Javier Martínez, a Democrat.

New Mexico's efforts put it at the vanguard of a popular, but expensive, concept on the left. New York City mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani, a Democratic socialist, wants universal child care, and has estimated it would cost $6 billion a year. To fund it, he will need help from state lawmakers to raise taxes.

There are logistical challenges to the New Mexico program, including the need for more daycare workers. Before November's policy change, there were 32,000 children in the state-sponsored program, and that number is expected to double by fiscal 2028. The state wants to add 5,000 more workers through recruitment, training and higher pay for workers at care centers that extend hours.

Opponents say the state risks crowding out lower-income families who need free care the most. "This is throwing money without a strategic plan and putting the burden on some families that will not be able to access the quality care or the programs that they need when they need it, " said Rebecca Dow, Republican caucus chair in the New Mexico House.

Until this month, New Mexico's child-care program had an income cap of just under $130,000 for a family of four. Now all families qualify independent of income, so long as parents are working or attending school. Families can still choose private-pay options if they want, and providers can also choose not to accept state contracts.

Because Stephanie Neeley and her husband make $170,000 combined, they have been paying out-of-pocket for their youngest of three children to attend A Gold Star Academy preschool in Farmington, N.M. They plan to apply for state coverage that Neeley said should save them about $7,000 on care before their child starts public school next year. The family can put that money toward emergency savings, said Neeley, who works in the energy industry.

The state's new policy also covers all grandparents who are primary caregivers, including Nancy Rodarte, who works with special-needs children in the Santa Fe, N.M., school system. Until now, Rodarte has relied on a scholarship from the local La Casita Preschool for her five-year-old granddaughter, Nova, to attend three days a week.

The new program should enable Rodarte to send her granddaughter to La Casita five days a week while relieving financial pressure on the school, because the state would cover the tab.

"She loves it here," Rodarte said, after arriving for pickup on a recent weekday while her granddaughter played on a swing set.

While free or subsidized universal child care is common in many parts of Europe, it is far less common in the U.S. Proponents in New Mexico want the program to make life easier for working parents but also help attract businesses and skilled workers. Income per capita in New Mexico is among the lowest in the nation, according to Commerce Department data.

"We were seeing families not wanting to have another child or families saying they had to move out of the state because they couldn't handle the child care costs," said Elizabeth Groginsky, New Mexico's cabinet secretary for Early Childhood Education.

The number of children under the age of 5 years old fell 11.3% in New Mexico from 2019 to 2024, while the number of residents aged 75 and older increased 19%, according to the state workforce department.

Having enough centers up and running, with enough staff, might be the state's biggest challenge. Even before the universal child-care policy kicked in, a state legislative report this summer said demand for child care "likely remains unmet." The report said capacity for children under two "continues to fall short of need, with only 32 child-care slots for every 100 children under the age of two in New Mexico."

To meet demand, the state hopes to license 55 additional child-care centers — with incentives including low-interest loans — and support 1,000 people to operate smaller daycares from their homes, which is needed in rural areas. It is mostly footing the bill via a unique revenue source: distributions from a fund built with oil-and-gas revenues boosted by the fracking boom. Even if an oil bust occurs, the program should be able to operate long-term by spending only the interest from the fund, according to the state.

Some child care providers are worried they will be squeezed by a universal state program.

Mary Matteucci, director of A Child's Garden in Albuquerque, said salaries and benefits already account for 90% of the preschool's expenses. But the new policy means the preschool will have to increase wages on its lowest earners to $19 an hour, or by about $3.50 an hour. She said this cost imbalance could lead to cuts rather than expanded care.

Anya Eckert, director of La Casita Preschool in Santa Fe, is worried the state's reimbursement rates might not match what her business charges for part-time care, which is in high demand. "We are at capacity," she said. "So it's not like we can take more children to make up the difference. We'll just be losing money."

Economists say growing pains are likely as the program rolls out, but offering workers higher pay should help. "Until we hit an equilibrium, I imagine there's going to be frictions where demand is going to overwhelm supply, but my hope is that the economy will respond," said Kira Villa, associate professor of economics at the University of New Mexico.

The state says the universal-care initiative is tackling the child-care shortage from various angles, starting with the areas where families struggle most — infant and toddler care, full-day options, and nontraditional hours. Groginsky, the cabinet secretary for the early childhood department, said, "We're really confident that our rates are at the same level or higher than what parents and what providers have been charging."

Jenna Cruz, 40, was already tapping the state program to send her 3.5-year-old son, Quentin, to La Casita part-time. She now plans to apply for a full-time slot so that she can work more hours. She owns her own mobile notary service, works at a medical office and delivers Amazon packages.

"I would be able to earn more money if he was able to be here full-time, " she said.

Write to Harriet Torry at harriet.torry@wsj.com and Dan Frosch at dan.frosch@wsj.com

data.title: In One of San Francisco's Priciest ZIPs, Buyers Are Moving Fast and Paying Cash — WSJ
data.description:
By Jessica Flint | Photography by Winni Wintermeyer for WSJ

San Francisco is back after pandemic-related struggles — and growing up. Affluent young families demanding space have elevated the 94127 ZIP Code, which is anchored by the walkable West Portal village center and encompasses desirable residential neighborhoods such as St. Francis Wood. This west side pocket reliably offers a rare San Francisco luxury: detached single-family homes with yards.

The suburbia-in-the-city lifestyle has recently propelled the ZIP Code to rival historically more expensive city enclaves. In October, its $2.5 million median listing price was second only to the $2.7 million median in premiere address Pacific Heights, according to Realtor.com. ( News Corp, owner of The Wall Street Journal, also operates Realtor.com.)

Drawing both new and multigenerational residents, 94127's appeal also lies in easy transit to downtown and the Peninsula and popular private school proximity.

West Portal Avenue, a roughly three-block stretch, is 94127's commercial core. Home to both new and legacy businesses, it's defined by a rail tunnel that provides a fast commute downtown.

Elena's

Founded in 2024, this Mexican-American spot is owned by the third-generation family behind Original Joe's, a San Francisco dining institution for more than eight decades.

Bookshop West Portal

This independent bookstore has been around since 2006. It hosts a lively calendar of events, including author talks and signings with major writers.

George's Donuts & Merriment

Opened in early 2025, this upscale cafe offers elevated doughnuts in addition to lunch fare. One of its co-founders is Hims & Hers telehealth CEO Andrew Dudum, who was born and raised in West Portal.

Guerra Quality Meats

This family-run butcher shop, established in 1954, is prized for its high-quality meats, deli selection and personal service. It is one of the few remaining old-school butchers in the city.

Classic 94127 hike

At 938 feet, Mount Davidson holds the distinction as San Francisco's highest natural point — a fact that often surprises people who mistakenly grant that title to Twin Peaks, further north and at 922 feet. This prominent, yet generally less-traveled summit is a favorite local hiking spot.

Membership to have

The Olympic Club sits close enough to 94127 for residents to consider it their own. Founded in 1860, it's one of the oldest athletic clubs in the country. It has two 18-hole championship courses and a 9-hole executive course. It also has a separate downtown clubhouse.

Advice for the buyer

Inventory in 94127 is limited, says Alexander Fromm Lurie, executive vice president of City Real Estate San Francisco. He recommends buyers work with an agent knowledgeable about off-market properties, as he says 20% to 30% of homes in this area are available privately before public listing. Additionally, Laurie suggests buyers prearrange financing to be able to act quickly. "A significant portion of sales are all-cash," he says, estimating that in San Francisco, more than 50% of homes above $5 million are cash transactions.

Median List Price $2.5 million (up 21% year-over-year)

Median days on market 37

Median price per square foot $952

Active Listings 16 (down 42% year-over-year)

Source: Realtor.com (October 2025)

This house with a curved facade was built around 1970. It is in the 94217 portion of the Forest Hills Extension neighborhood. There are six bedrooms on a single level, including a primary suite with ocean views. The 2,766-square-foot lower level has an entertainment room and two guest rooms. There is also a four car garage and elevator to all five levels. Agent: Neal Ward, Compass.

This detached Mediterranean-style home is in the St. Francis Wood neighborhood. Built in 1925, it has recently been fully renovated. The kitchen on the first floor opens to a dining area and family room. Upstairs, the primary suite has dual vanities and a soaking tub. There is a lower-level suite with a private entry. Agent: Dale T. Boutiette, Compass.

Write to Jessica Flint at Jessica.Flint@wsj.com

data.title: Decidr to Acquire Sugarwork to Accelerate Knowledge Capture in the Agentic Economy
data.description:
(17:00 GMT) *Decidr To Acquire Sugarwork To Accelerate Knowledge Capture In The Agentic Economy >DAI.AU
(17:00 GMT) Press Release: Decidr to Acquire Sugarwork to Accelerate Knowledge Capture in the Agentic Economy

NEW YORK--(BUSINESS WIRE)--November 19, 2025--

Decidr AI Industries Ltd (, "Decidr"), an agentic technology company on the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX), today announced it has entered into a binding agreement to acquire Sugarwork: a US-based AI software platform that captures and activates operational knowledge inside businesses. The acquisition into Decidr US. Inc, a wholly owned subsidiary of DAI marks an important strategic step in accelerating Decidr's vision of creating a Networked Agentic Economy, an ecosystem of intelligent, AI-organizations that are linked and can think and act independently.

Founded in 2022 and headquartered in New York, Sugarwork began by helping organizations capture and own their tacit institutional knowledge. With businesses now adopting AI, but not having a clear view of their underlying workflows, Sugarwork has since evolved into a full-spectrum discovery platform that maps the invisible 80% of knowledge inside a business: pinpointing where to invest in automation and AI, and surfacing previously undocumented workflows.

Sugarwork's technology transforms hidden human knowledge into structured, actionable processes. Integrating this capability will allow Decidr to convert that intelligence into self-learning systems - the foundation of truly autonomous, adaptive organizations, and what Decidr coins as the Agentic Economy.

"We're building the engine that powers the Agentic Economy," said David Brudenell, Executive Chair and Co-CEO of Decidr. "With Sugarwork, businesses can quickly uncover hidden knowledge - in many cases the most valuable type of knowledge and see it in a structured way. This exercise making the invisible visible is a powerful starting point for Agentic transformation using Decidr."

Decidr will embed Sugarwork's knowledge activation tools into the core DecidrOS onboarding experience, reducing onboarding time for agentic organizations and improving schema precision. Sugarwork will continue to be available as a standalone discovery and transformation product, supporting partners and customers with business transformation and before investment in AI is made, as they prepare for Agentification.

Sugarwork's platform has already delivered strong results for clients across sectors, including reducing onboarding time by 70% for global AI data provider Appen, and helped surface enterprise-critical processes for a USD$800 million Nasdaq-listed medtech company.

Vanessa Liu, CEO of Sugarwork, said: "We founded Sugarwork to solve a problem every organization faces: knowledge lives in people's heads. Decidr shares our belief that unlocking this knowledge is the gateway to meaningful, scalable AI. Joining forces accelerates our mission further and faster, giving teams a clear path from discovery to intelligent execution."

Sugarwork will continue to operate under its existing brand, with added support from Decidr's technical and commercial teams. Existing Sugarwork customers will gain access to Decidr's partner ecosystem and innovation roadmap, while new and prospective DecidrOS clients will benefit from an accelerated path to schema-based intelligence.

This acquisition follows Decidr's recent A$20 million capital raise, and marks another step in building out the tools, partnerships and infrastructure required to deliver agentic AI at global scale. Together, Decidr and Sugarwork will accelerate onboarding for customers, deepen insights for automation, and bring the Agentic Economy closer to reality.

Completion of the transaction remains subject to customary closing conditions, including execution of a definitive merger implementation agreement, Decidr undertaking due diligence, and the parties obtaining any shareholder and regulatory approvals in both the US and Australia. The acquisition is expected to complete by December 31, 2025.

Registrations for the DecidrOS are now open, find out more here.

About Decidr

Built on two decades of research into human behaviour, Decidr is pioneering the world's first Agentic Operating System for businesses. The DecidrOS platform seamlessly connects a company's existing data and tools, using coordinated AI apps to automate everyday work with unprecedented speed and accuracy.

Unlike standalone AI features that operate in silos, Decidr provides a trusted, deterministic orchestration layer that transforms fragmented workflows into seamless, intelligent processes across all business functions--from sales and finance to HR and marketing. This revolutionary approach enables SMEs to achieve enterprise-level automation and efficiency without the complexity or cost traditionally associated with digital transformation.

In October 2025, Decidr was recognised as one of Australia's most innovative companies by the Australian Financial Review, listed under both professional services and AI engineering.

About Sugarwork

Founded in 2022 and headquartered in New York, Sugarwork is a New York-based software company that captures and activates operational knowledge inside businesses. Originally built to enable organizations to capture and own their tacit institutional knowledge, Sugarwork now empowers organizations to discover cross-functional and business-wide insights, map workflows, and reveal automation opportunities to prepare organizations for AI adoption. Clients span financial services, manufacturing, healthcare and professional services.

Connections, Local Flavors, and Luxurious Experiences Top the 2026 Bacardi Cocktail Trends Report
data.description:
This year's findings highlight a move towards more meaningful occasions, micro-indulgence, and storytelling cocktail experiences

HAMILTON, BERMUDA / ACCESS Newswire / November 19, 2025 / The five defining trends set to reshape cocktail culture and the spirits industry in 2026 are highlighted in the seventh annual Bacardi Cocktail Trends Report. The Report released by Bacardi Limited, the largest privately held international spirits company, in partnership with The Future Laboratory (TFL), draws on data from Bacardi-led and third-party research, consumer surveys, bartender interviews and TFL's trend forecasting to uncover the forces shaping cocktail experiences, flavor innovation, and drinking culture in the year ahead. The Report also reveals the cocktails which are set to be the most popular in 2026 and trends for younger Legal Drinking Age (LDA) consumers.

Gen Z isn't drinking less, they're simply drinking earlier, lighter, and with more intention. Around the world, we're seeing a move towards more meaningful drinking moments whether that means in-person get-togethers with friends, discovering flavors rooted in local culture, or embracing new forms of creative expression through cocktails."

Sean Kerry, Vice President for Global On-Trade for Bacardi

Consumers are moving from curating experiences to cultivating connections. The pendulum has swung from digital convenience to human creativity, and the drinks industry sits at the center of that shift. In 2026, value will be defined not by scarcity or status, but by depth: the provenance of ingredients, the stories behind serves, and the ability to transform a moment into meaning."

Martin Raymond, Co-Founder of The Future Laboratory

Here are the macro-trends defining the spirits industry in 2026:

  • AFTERNOON SOCIETY 

    Happy hour is having a cultural renaissance as earlier-day indulgence replaces late-night excess. The rise of "daycaps" (cocktails enjoyed in the late afternoon to close the workday) marks a shift toward micro-celebrations that fit modern routines. Across regions, people are heading out to drink, eat, and socialize earlier in the evenings. Notably, younger LDA consumers are leading the charge, with over half of those in France (51%) and more than one third in the U.S. (34%) reshaping routines around earlier evenings, according to the Bacardi Global Consumer Survey (GCS). Daytime drinking now revolves around Spritz culture, mood-based cocktails, and small serves that tap into the sweet treat economy. It's not about escape; it's about new rhythm and new routines of enjoyment that fit your energy and your calendar.

  • REWILDING CONNECTION

    As social life recalibrates away from constant connectivity, drinkers are seeking intentional, offline experiences that prioritize presence over performance. With 84% of consumers saying technology has made social interactions feel less personal, bars and brands are designing shared, analog moments that feel human again-think screen-free gatherings, communal serves, analog entertainment, and rituals that spark real conversation. From martini flights to micro-format gatherings, Rewilding Connection reflects a consumer desire to slow down, tune back in, and rediscover the social magic of enjoying a cocktail together.

  • NEW LOCALOGY

    As changing trade conditions and transparency reshape the industry, bars are evolving into laboratories of local flavor - harnessing micro-farms, regional ecologies, and scientific experimentation to pioneer a new future of mixology. This movement is not about replacing internationally celebrated spirits, but about elevating them through locally sourced ingredients, garnishes, and flavor accents that root each serve in its environment. Here, every sip is as much about terroir and technique as it is about transparency - catering to new consumer preferences to savor not only the flavor, but the place and process behind it. In fact, three-quarters (77%) check ingredient origin labels, seeking locally sourced ingredients.

  • THE LIQUID EXPERIENCE IP

    No longer just vessels for a taste experience, cocktails and drinks brands are transforming into full spectrum lifestyle experiences that blend fashion, music, design, travel and sport into cohesive cultural identities. Gen Z and millennials choose brands that reflect their identity, and 70% of people who say emotional engagement drives loyalty. Bars and brands are responding with immersive cocktail worlds, drink characters, playlist pairings, traveling pop-up menus, and branded sensory elements like scent, sound, and storytelling. In 2026, a cocktail isn't just ordered; it's experienced, collected, and followed like a creator brand.

  • MORE IS MORE MIXOLOGY

    After years of "quiet luxury" minimalism, maximalism is back behind the bar with showstopping cocktails full of glamor and theatrics. Over three quarters of the Bacardi GCS participants (76%) value heightened, memorable experiences as bars embrace edible pearls, metallic garnishes, evolving flavor layers, fire presentations, and high-drama glassware. Loud luxury venues-from Dubai to Las Vegas-prove that joyfully excessive design and unapologetic opulence are in. In a world still healing from burnout, people don't just want a drink, they want a moment.

Globally, the top 10 cocktails in 2026 will be:

  • Margarita

  • Mojito

  • Piña Colada

  • Rum and Coke®

  • Whisky and Coke®

  • Spritz

  • Vodka Lemonade

  • Vodka Soda

  • Gin & Tonic

  • Dry Martini Cocktail

Download the complete 2026 Bacardi Cocktail Trends Report and Infographic.

About Bacardi Limited

Bacardi Limited, the world's largest privately held international spirits company, produces, markets, and distributes spirits and wines. The Bacardi Limited portfolio comprises more than 200 brands and labels, including BACARDÍ® rum, PATRÓN® tequila, GREY GOOSE® vodka, DEWAR'S® Blended Scotch whisky, BOMBAY SAPPHIRE® gin, MARTINI® vermouth and sparkling wines, CAZADORES® 100% blue agave tequila, and other leading and emerging brands including D'USSÉ® Cognac, ANGEL'S ENVY® American straight whiskey, and ST-GERMAIN® elderflower liqueur. Founded more than 163 years ago in Santiago de Cuba, family-owned Bacardi Limited currently employs approximately 8,000, operates production facilities in 10 countries and territories, and sells its brands in more than 160 markets. Bacardi Limited refers to the Bacardi group of companies, including Bacardi International Limited. Visit http://www.bacardilimited.com or follow us on LinkedIn and Instagram.

data.title: Can Chinese-Made Buses Be Hacked? Norway Drove One Down a Mine to Find Out — WSJ
data.description:
By Stephen Wilmot and Ed Ballard

This summer, Oslo's public-transport authority drove a Chinese electric bus deep into a decommissioned mine inside a nearby mountain to answer a question: Could it be hacked?

Isolated by rock from digital interference, cybersecurity experts came back with a qualified yes: The bus could in theory be remotely disabled using the control system for the battery.

The revelation, presented at a recent public-transport conference, has spurred officials in Denmark and the U.K. to start their own investigations into Chinese vehicles. It has also fed into broader security concerns across Europe about the growing prevalence of Chinese-made equipment in the region's energy and telecommunications infrastructure.

The worry is the same for autos, solar panels and other connected devices: that mechanisms used for wirelessly delivering system updates could also be exploited by a hostile government or third-party hacker to compromise critical networks.

"Norway and Denmark alerted us to the existence of dual-use kill switches in Chinese-made electric buses. These switches allow China to switch off buses and bring chaos to transport systems," British lawmaker Alicia Kearns warned during a debate this week on Chinese security risks.

In the U.S., Chinese technology is heavily restricted in telecommunications networks and autos, though Chinese buses are still permitted. Chinese solar power equipment has attracted scrutiny too.

In Europe, tensions are coming to the surface as policymakers upgrade their arsenal of digital defenses. Recently introduced cybersecurity legislation in the U.K. would make companies in critical sectors including energy liable to be fined if they don't prepare for attacks from hackers or state actors.

The Oslo transport authority, Ruter, said the bus's mobile-network connection via a Romanian SIM card gave manufacturer Yutong access to the control system for battery and power supply. Ruter said it is addressing the vulnerability by developing firewalls and delaying the signals sent to the vehicles, among other solutions.

Ruter's investigation was partly inspired by tests conducted by Tor Indstøy, an employee of Norwegian telecommunications company Telenor who spent his spare time investigating his family car — made by China's NIO — as part of what he called "Project Lion Cage."

Yutong was the bestselling electric-bus manufacturer in Europe last year. Chinese carmaker BYD is another big supplier.

Yutong said it stored data from vehicles sold in Europe at a data center in Germany managed by Amazon Web Services, and got consent from customers before delivering over-the-air updates.

China's embassy in Norway warned against "hyping up" theoretical risks. "Overstretching the concept of security only ends up hindering competition and innovation, rather than blocking risks," it said.

Security concerns are also growing about energy infrastructure, with Europe's push into green power increasingly underpinned by low-cost Chinese equipment.

Around 30 members of the European Parliament wrote to Europe's top cybersecurity and energy officials last month to warn that inverters — devices that hook solar panels up to the grid — could be exploited to disrupt the power system.

"We are one software update away from disaster," said Bart Groothuis, a lawmaker from the Netherlands who is leading the campaign.

The lawmakers want to reduce the region's reliance on Chinese-made inverters, which dominate the European market. The market leader is Huawei Technologies, whose telecommunications hardware is subject to restrictions in some European countries.

Roughly a dozen inverter suppliers — not all Chinese — have remote access to enough solar panels in Europe for a compromise of their systems to have a "significant impact on the stability of the grid," according to estimates by risk-management company DNV for trade association SolarPower Europe. The trade group suggested measures such as ensuring inverters are controlled in the European Union or other trusted jurisdictions.

A blackout that paralyzed Spain and Portugal in April showed how a relatively small glitch on the grid, such as an outage at a few power plants, can cascade.

Lithuania last year passed legislation blocking manufacturers from countries deemed to pose a national-security threat — including China — from having remote access to larger solar installations.

But politicians around Europe are also weighing cybersecurity risks against the potential benefits of China's low-cost equipment and investments.

In Scotland, Ming Yang Smart Energy plans to invest up to roughly $2 billion in a wind-turbine factory. British lawmaker Christine Jardine said she wants an outcome that allows the plan to go ahead without "giving a potentially hostile country the on-off switch to the power in the North Sea."

The plan is waiting for approvals. A U.K. government spokesman said any decision would "be consistent with our national security." Ming Yang said last month it was targeting "the highest levels of data protection and cybersecurity."

EU officials are also working on strengthening cybersecurity rules. A "ProtectEU" strategy published earlier this year noted "unacceptable security risks" in Europe's 5G networks.

The EU in 2020 recommended that member countries restrict equipment from 5G suppliers deemed to pose a security risk, and in 2023 identified Huawei as a high-risk supplier. But the company's gear remains widely used.

"A lack of swift action exposes the EU as a whole to a clear risk," said a spokesperson for the European Commission, the bloc's executive arm.

The ProtectEU strategy also identified critical sectors of concern beyond 5G networks, including electricity, renewable energy and connected vehicles.

While European cars don't typically include Chinese software, Chinese electric vehicles are rapidly gaining traction in the region, many brimming with sensors and connected to the internet through central computers.

"The same thing has happened with cars as with 5G," said Valentin Weber, a senior research fellow at the German Council on Foreign Relations. "10 years later we'll have to rip and replace at great cost because we weren't early enough with our restrictions and bans."

Write to Stephen Wilmot at stephen.wilmot@wsj.com and Ed Ballard at ed.ballard@wsj.com

data.title: Bitcoin Drops Below $90K — WSJ
data.description:
By Vicky Ge Huang

Bitcoin fell below $90,000 on Wednesday, with investors continuing to pull back from speculative and riskier corners of the market.

The largest cryptocurrency has erased its year-to-date gains and now trades about 30% off its all-time high set on Oct.6.

Analysts have said that the uncertainty surrounding the Federal Reserve's interest-rate policy and heavy selling activity across institutional and individual investors are weighing on token prices.

On Tuesday, investors yanked more than half a billion dollars from BlackRock's iShares Bitcoin Trust, the exchange-traded fund's largest single-day outflow since inception.

data.title: Law firm Pillsbury faces class action over April data breach
data.description:
By Mike Scarcella

U.S. law firm Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman is facing a proposed class action on behalf of thousands of people whose sensitive personal data was allegedly compromised by a cyberattack on the firm in April.

The lawsuit, filed on Tuesday in federal court in Manhattan by a Texas resident, said the breach exposed names, Social Security numbers, addresses, dates of birth and financial account details. It alleges Pillsbury failed in its duty to safeguard the personal data and to promptly notify affected individuals, leaving them vulnerable to identity theft and fraud.

Pillsbury, which has offices in Los Angeles, Washington, New York and other cities, disclosed the breach on November 6, according to the complaint. The 700-lawyer firm had no immediate comment. Attorneys for the plaintiff did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

In a public notice about the data breach, Pillsbury said the attack came from “sophisticated social engineering,” a technique that involves duping people into disclosing sensitive information, and that "an unauthorized actor was able to convince a single user to enable the actor to download some firm documents in a short period of time."

Large law firms regularly possess protected data related to their clients, employees and litigation adversaries. The lawsuit did not explain why Pillsbury had the plaintiff's personal information. It said the firm "solicited, collected, used, and derived a benefit from the private information, yet breached its duty by failing to implement or maintain adequate security practices."

Pillsbury, in a notice to a state attorney general's office, said it blocked the activity and took steps to deploy additional computer safeguards. “The firm is committed to safeguarding its systems and information,” it said in the notice.

The complaint seeks nationwide class action status and damages of more than $5 million.

Prominent law firm Williams & Connolly in October said hackers had gained access to some of its systems. The firm said then that “there is no evidence that confidential client data was extracted from any other part of our I.T. system, including from databases where client files are stored.”

Last year, law firm Orrick agreed to pay $8 million to settle a lawsuit over a breach that allegedly compromised the personal data of more than 600,000 people. Orrick is representing Pillsbury in matters related to its breach.

The case is Allison Archer v. Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman, U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York, No. 1:25-cv-09613

For plaintiff: Mark Svensson and Mariya Weekes of Milberg

For defendant: No appearances yet

data.title: Antea Group Welcomes Dr. Jase Hixson as a Senior Consultant
data.description:
ST. PAUL, MN / ACCESS Newswire / November 19, 2025 / Antea Group USA is pleased to announce the addition of Jase Hixson, Ph.D., as a Senior Consultant supporting and advancing our water and wastewater management services. Dr. Hixson brings more than fifteen years of experience in water and wastewater treatment operations, hydrologic modeling, and emerging contaminant assessment across municipal, industrial, and natural water systems.

Dr. Hixson's career spans a unique combination of academic research, field operations, and consulting. He has operated and advised wastewater treatment facilities ranging from 200 gallons per day to more than 85 million gallons per day, optimizing biological treatment through a mass-balance approach, microscopic diagnostics, and adaptive operating strategies. His work has helped municipalities and industry achieve regulatory compliance, reduce operational costs, and improve system performance and sustainability.

In addition to his operational expertise, Dr. Hixson has extensive experience with PFAS and other emerging contaminants. He began working with PFAS more than a decade ago, researching the environmental fate of PFAS used in "forever pesticides" in the Great Lakes. Since then, he has supported investigations, remediation strategies, and emergency response efforts involving PFAS releases, applying advanced surface water, groundwater, and coupled hydrologic models to assess fate, transport, and system recovery.

Dr. Hixson has constructed and evaluated hundreds of hydrologic models to characterize the movement of water and solutes across diverse environments. These efforts have supported projects involving PFAS, nutrients, harmful algal blooms (HABs), petroleum constituents, VOCs, acids, caustics, heavy metals, pesticides, herbicides, and wastewater-derived emerging contaminants.

Internationally, he has led and supported projects across the United States, Canada, Africa, and China, collaborating with municipalities, regulatory agencies, research institutions, and private-sector clients. His work has included wastewater surveillance program development during the peak of COVID-19, PFAS mass-balance modeling to support community water protection, and rapid treatability testing for emergency wastewater treatment response.

"We are thrilled to welcome Jase to our team," said Alizabeth Aramowicz Smith, Vice President and Environment, Health & Safety Solution and Innovation Leader at Antea Group. "His deep understanding of wastewater operations combined with his experience in emerging contaminants and hydrologic modeling positions him as a tremendous resource for our clients. Jase brings a rare blend of scientific rigor, practical operational insight, and collaborative problem-solving. We are excited for the leadership and technical excellence he brings to our wastewater services portfolio."

Dr. Hixson's primary areas of expertise include:

  • Wastewater treatment optimization and compliance strategy

  • Surface water and groundwater hydrology

  • Environmental fate and transport modeling

  • Microbial and algal community analysis

  • PFAS and emerging contaminant assessment and remediation

  • Technical expert support and testimony

He joins Antea Group as we continue to expand our support for municipalities, utilities, water-intensive industries, and organizations addressing regulatory uncertainty, sustainability goals, and complex water quality challenges.

Learn more about our wastewater services here.

About Antea Group 

Antea®Group is an environment, health, safety, and sustainability consulting firm. By combining strategic thinking with technical expertise, we do more than effectively solve client challenges; we deliver sustainable results for a better future. We work in partnership with and advise many of the world's most sustainable companies to address ESG business challenges in a way that fits their pace and unique objectives. Our consultants equip organizations to better understand threats, capture opportunities, and find their position of strength. Lastly, we maintain a global perspective on ESG issues through not only our work with multinational clients, but also through our sister organizations in Europe, Asia, and Latin America and as a founding member of the Inogen Alliance. Learn more at us.anteagroup.com.

data.title: Seabridge Gold's KSM Mining Awarded the AME David Barr Award for Excellence in Health & Safety Leadership & Innovation
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - November 19, 2025) - Seabridge Gold announced today that its subsidiary KSM Mining ULC has received the AME David Barr Award for excellence in mineral exploration health and safety. The award recognizes our leadership and innovation in developing a collaborative approach to health and safety which has brought together companies and communities in the Golden Triangle region of Northwest B.C.

The annual AME Awards celebrate remarkable individual and team achievements, contributions and innovation in mineral exploration, and recognize excellence in geoscience, prospecting, financing, social and environmental responsibility, health and safety, innovation and outstanding service to AME and the mineral exploration and development community.

KSM Mining earned its distinction for its pioneering efforts to foster collaboration and enhance emergency preparedness across British Columbia's remote Golden Triangle region, in the Critical Conservation Corridor. In fall 2023, we launched the Golden Triangle Emergency Response Coordination Meeting, a monthly forum bringing together emergency response and safety professionals from multiple projects and organizations.

Rudi Fronk, Seabridge CHAIR and CEO: "I am very proud of our team for establishing a collaborative approach to regional safety capabilities, ensuring a unified and effective response to emergencies, including supporting community emergency responses. This effort exemplifies our core values of integrity, excellence and family. Innovative initiatives like this help to improve our industry's performance and enhance its standing in the community. My thanks to everyone involved for your dedication and teamwork. Your actions are a shining example of commitment to safety and community."

AME President and CEO Todd Stone said: "On behalf of the AME Board of Directors and the entire mineral exploration community, we congratulate and thank all 2025 AME Award recipients. Their passion, commitment and leadership exemplify the spirit of mineral exploration, and our community's drive to continuously improve how we search for, discover and develop the minerals that are essential to our future."

The AME Awards are presented at the annual AME Awards Gala - A Celebration of Excellence, which takes place on January 28, 2026, at the Vancouver Convention Centre West.

About AMEThe Association for Mineral Exploration (AME) is the lead association for the mineral exploration and development industry based in British Columbia. Established in 1912, AME represents, advocates and promotes the interests of more than 6,000 members who are engaged in mineral exploration and development in BC and globally. AME encourages a safe, economically strong and environmentally responsible industry by providing clear initiatives, policies, events and tools to support its membership in delivering responsible projects that advance reconciliation and provide benefit to all British Columbians.

About Seabridge GoldSeabridge holds a 100% interest in several North American gold projects. Seabridge's principal asset, the KSM project, and its Iskut projects are located in Northwest British Columbia, Canada's "Golden Triangle", the Courageous Lake project is in Canada's Northwest Territories, the Snowstorm project in the Getchell Gold Belt of Northern Nevada and the 3 Aces project is in the Yukon Territory. For a full breakdown of Seabridge's Mineral Reserves and Mineral Resources by category please visit Seabridge's website at http://www.seabridgegold.com.

data.title: Bangladesh court halts arbitration with India's Adani Power over dues
data.description:
By Ruma Paul and Sethuraman N R

The Bangladesh High Court on Wednesday halted arbitration proceedings between the country's power development board and India's Adani Power in Singapore on disputes around power supply payments.

The company, led by Indian billionaire Gautam Adani, has been at loggerheads with the Bangladesh Power Development Board over pending payments for the electricity it supplies as part of a pact that both sides had signed in 2017.

Adani and the BPDB had early this month decided to go for an international arbitration process to resolve disputes over Bangladesh's power supply payments.

The court said on Wednesday that the arbitration will remain suspended until the report of a high court-formed committee to investigate the fairness and possible irregularities in the agreement between the Bangladesh government and the Adani Group is submitted.

The Bangladesh High court last year had ordered an examination of the contract with Adani by a committee of experts.

Adani Power supplies electricity from its coal-fired 1,600 megawatt Godda power plant in eastern India, which meets nearly a tenth of Bangladesh's power needs.

"If Adani starts arbitration proceedings in Singapore over their dues before the investigation report comes, then the investigation will have no importance," Abdul Qayyum, a lawyer who filed the injunction petition, told reporters.

The interim government in Bangladesh had accused Adani of breaching the power purchase agreement by withholding tax benefits that the Godda plant got from India, Reuters reported in December.

Bangladesh paid Adani a tariff of 14.87 taka ($0.1220) per unit during the fiscal year to June 30, 2024, higher than an average of 9.57 taka for power supplied by other Indian companies.

An Adani Group spokesperson said in a statement that while the company was yet to review the order, as per the agreement, dispute between the parties have to be resolved through Singapore International Arbitration Centre, which is not subject to jurisdiction of Bangladesh courts.

BPDB did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

data.title: The AI Bubble May Have Already Popped. The Rally Can Still Survive. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Martin Baccardax

It wasn't too long ago that the mere mention of "artificial intelligence" in a corporate press release would trigger an upside move for the stock.

Investors, eager to get ahead of the next wave of the new technology's potential, were happy to believe in, and bet on, a bottom-line impact from AI deployment — even if it couldn't be fully explained.

We're not there now.

Target attempted to sweeten the impact of a mixed set of third-quarter earnings and a dour holiday season outlook by floating a new partnership with OpenAI. The struggling retailer vowed to "leverage AI to help guests explore our assortment with ease."

The stock barely budged.

Companies at the center of the AI investment boom are also finding it tough to secure a sympathetic ear for their lofty new tech ambitions.

Meta Platforms shares have fallen more than 20% since its third-quarter update in late October detailed massive capital spending and expenses commitment tied to its pursuit of "superintelligence."

Oracle has slumped nearly 33% since it unveiled a massive increase in its RPO forecast that was subsequently tied to a $300 billion deal with OpenAI. Smaller players in the AI space, such as CoreWeave and Palantir Technologies, have also seen selloffs.

Nvidia, meanwhile, is largely even with the S&P 500's 3% gain since it last reported earnings on Aug. 27. Investors are bullish on the market, but also starting to question the scale and pace of AI investments after one of the worst November slumps since 2008.

Stocks moving in the other direction, by contrast, are doing so largely as a result of faith in their core businesses as opposed to the discounted returns on AI spending.

Google shares have soared nearly 43% since a U.S. court ruled on Sept. 2 that its parent company, Alphabet, could keep its Chrome browser and Android operating system despite having lost a landmark antitrust case in 2024.

The world's most famous value investor, Warren Buffett, recently went along for the ride.

Apple, which has failed to capture investor imagination with its long-game AI strategy, has risen around 34% since late summer off the back of robust iPhone demand that helped generate a record $102.5 billion in revenue over the three months ended in September.

Put simply, while the biggest stocks in the AI trade still dominate index performance with the sheer heft of their market values, investors are separating the wheat from the chaff in the recent pullback. And that likely means that AI will no longer be the proverbial tide lifting all boats in the stock market.

But it doesn't mean the AI rally is over. It may just evolve differently.

Jean Boivin, who heads the BlackRock Investment Institute, sees the capex increases from the sector's biggest players as a "necessary step in the AI buildout," but notes that earnings outside of tech are also showing big improvements.

Magnificent Seven profits for the third quarter, not counting Nvidia, rose 27% from last year, but the rest of the S&P 500 saw much better-than-expected gains of 14%.

That can pull new investment money away from the speculative AI themes and into more traditional, income generating stocks, lowering overall market multiples at the same time.

John Belton, a portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, also notes that the forward price/earnings multiple for the Mag 7 has already contracted by around 10% since the start of the year, even as an index tracking the group's performance is up nearly 20%.

"That implies that earnings growth, rather than multiple expansion, has propelled share price performance," he argues, adding that only two stocks in the group, Alphabet and Tesla, have seen their forward P/E ratios increase this year.

"This is good evidence that while earnings estimates are always subject to negative revisions, it is difficult to argue that we are in a valuation bubble currently," he said.

And perhaps a good indication that the broader AI bubble may have already popped.

Write to Martin Baccardax at martin.baccardax@barrons.com

data.title: Germany – TotalEnergies offshore wind on time
data.description:
By Rod Morrison

TotalEnergies has submitted permit applications for the first two of its 7.5GW offshore wind projects to the Federal Maritime & Hydrographic Agency – the 2GW NordseeEnergies 1 scheme in the North Sea, site N-12.1, and the 1GW OstseeEnergies project in the Baltic Sea, site O-2.2.

Last month it was reported economy minister Katherina Reiche had turned down a request from TotalEnergies CEO Patrick Pouyanne to improve the financial terms agreed when the French major won the schemes in summer 2023. Under the deal TotalEnergies will pay €5.8bn for the right to build the schemes. In contrast, the company won the 1GW site N-9.4 North Sea tender this summer with a bid of €180m.

The permit applications for N-12.1 and O-2.2 have been submitted on time. Construction is due to begin in 2029 with completion due in 2031.

data.title: PLATTS: 400--US Western power prices rise on lower renewables, high demand - 19Nov25
data.description: (Platts)--19Nov2025/1156 am EST/1656 GMT Wholesale western on-peak power prices traded mostly higher on Nov. 19 on the Intercontinental Exchange, driven by lower renewables and high demand PRICES In the Pacific Northwest, Mid-C on-peak power for Nov. 20 delivery rose about $3.75 to a price near $55.25/MWh, while its off-peak counterpart was the only package decreasing over the day. It dropped by around $4.25 to $48/MWh. In the California Independent System Operator, SP15 and NP15 day-ahead on-peak prices averaged about $3.75 higher, around $47.50/MWh and $58/MWh, respectively. Meanwhile, Southwest's Palo Verde rose by $5.25 from the previous trading session, trading around $46.50/MWh. Its off-peak counterpart followed the same trend and increased by about $7 to $52.25/MWh MARKET FUNDAMENTALS Providing upward pressure, CAISO's Nov. 20 peak load demand was forecast to rise 1.57% from the day before to 27.21 GW. Adding to higher prices, solar renewables were expected to decrease by around 4.87%, moving from 70.87 GW Nov. 19 to 67.42 GW Nov. 20, according to CAISO's forecasts. In the Northwest, average daily inflows at the Grand Coulee Dam were forecast to drop 2.2% to about 88,536 cubic feet per second, according to the Northwest River Forecast Center. The Southwest expected lower peakload demand in the Arizona-New Mexico-Southern Nevada Region, decreasing from 14.9 GW Nov. 19 to 14.73 GW on Nov. 20, according to Platts peakload demand forecast data. Most temperatures in the West were expected to be around their five-day norm, Los Angeles highs were forecast to reach 60 F (16 C), 10 F below their norm, and Phoenix was forecast to reach 63 F, 14 F below its norm, according to CustomWeather. In natural gas pricing, the SoCal and PG&E city-gates fell by 12 cents and 25 cents from the prior Platts assessments, trading at $4.19/MMBtu and $4.15/MMBtu, respectively. Platts is part of S&P Global Energy. — Mauricio Avila, mauricio.avila@spglobal.com-- Edited by Derek Sands, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--
